Austral Ecology




   RESEARCH ARTICLE



Day-ВӯNight Dynamics in the Organisation of AntвҖ“Plant
Interaction Networks in a Brazilian Amazon Rainforest
PatrГӯcia Nakayama Miranda1    | Maiara de Souza Bento1                       | ГҚtalo Miranda Armes1            | Izaias Brasil da Silva2   |
Ricardo Eduardo Vicente3   | FabrГӯcio Beggiato Baccaro3,4                      | Wesley DГЎttilo5
1Campus Rio Branco, Instituto Federal do Acre, Rio Branco, Acre, Brasil| 2LaboratГіrio de BotГўnica e Ecologia Vegetal, Universidade Federal do Acre,
Rio Branco, Acre, Brasil | 3Departamento de Biologia, Universidade Federal do Amazonas, Manaus, Amazonas, Brasil | 4CoordenaГ§ГЈo de Tecnologia
e InovaГ§ГЈo, Instituto Nacional de Pesquisas da AmazГҙnia, Manaus, Amazonas, Brasil | 5Red de EcoetologГӯa, Instituto de EcologГӯa, A.C, Xalapa,
Veracruz, MГ©xico


Correspondence: PatrГӯcia Nakayama Miranda (patricia.miranda@ifac.edu.br)

Received: 21 August 2025 | Revised: 16 February 2026 | Accepted: 10 March 2026

Keywords: beta diversity of interactions | complex networks | extrafloral nectaries | Formicidae | tropical forest

Palavras-chave: diversidade beta de interaГ§Гөes | nectГЎrios extraflorais | Formicidae | redes complexas | floresta tropical


  ABSTRACT
  Interactions between ants and plants bearing extrafloral nectaries (EFN-Вӯbearing plants) represent a classic plant defence strategy
  that involves a high diversity of species in tropical forests. Several studies have investigated the structuring processes of these
  antвҖ“plant interactions across spatial and temporal scales. However, surprisingly little is known about how these interactions vary
  throughout the day. In this study, we investigated daily variations in antвҖ“plant interactions mediated by extrafloral nectaries
  in a Brazilian tropical forest, using a complex network approach and beta-Вӯdiversity analysis of interactions. We sampled these
  antвҖ“plant interactions in nine 150 Г— 10 m plots over three periods of the day (morning, afternoon and night). We organised the
  data from each period into quantitative matrices and combined data from two and three periods to investigate possible increases
  in network descriptors. We calculated the total beta diversity of interactions, as well as its additive components (species turno-
  ver and interaction rewiring), across different pairs of periods. We found that network specialisation and nestedness remained
  constant throughout the day. However, at night, the networks exhibited reductions in network size, the number of interactions
  and diversity of interactions. Networks combining data from three periods showed pronounced increases in network size and
  the number of interactions compared with two-Вӯand single-Вӯperiod networks, suggesting temporal niche partitioning associated
  with extrafloral nectaries. Compositional changes were also detected through the beta diversity of interactions. Greater species
  turnover and interaction rewiring occurred between the nocturnal period and both diurnal periods. Our results underscore the
  critical role of the daily temporal scale in structuring these mutualistic networks and reveal how distinct ant foraging strategies
  partition the resource in time, cautioning against ecological interpretations derived solely from diurnal sampling.

  RESUMO
  InteraГ§Гөes entre formigas e plantas portadoras de nectГЎrios extraflorais representam uma clГЎssica estratГ©gia de defesa de plan-
  tas, que envolve uma elevada diversidade de espГ©cies em florestas tropicais. Diversos estudos tГӘm investigado sobre os processos
  estruturadores destas interaГ§Гөes no espaГ§o e no tempo. No entanto, sabemos relativamente pouco sobre como essas interaГ§Гөes
  mudam ao longo do dia. Neste estudo, investigamos variaГ§Гөes diГЎrias nas interaГ§Гөes formiga-Вӯplanta mediadas por nectГЎrios ex-
  traflorais em uma floresta tropical brasileira, usando uma abordagem de redes complexas e anГЎlises de diversidade beta de inter-
  aГ§Гөes. Amostramos essas interaГ§Гөes em nove parcelas de 150 Г— 10 m em trГӘs perГӯodos do dia (manhГЈ, tarde e noite). Organizamos

See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
  os dados de cada perГӯodo em matrizes quantitativas e combinamos dados de dois e trГӘs perГӯodos para investigar possГӯveis aumen-
  tos nos descritores de rede. Calculamos a diversidade beta total de interaГ§Гөes, bem como seus componentes aditivos (substituiГ§ГЈo
  de espГ©cies e reconfiguraГ§ГЈo de interaГ§Гөes) entre diferentes pares de perГӯodos. Constatamos que a especializaГ§ГЈo e o aninha-
  mento das redes permaneceram constantes ao longo do dia. Contudo, Г  noite, as redes exibiram reduГ§Гөes no tamanho, nГәmero
  de interaГ§Гөes e diversidade de interaГ§Гөes. Redes com dados combinados de trГӘs perГӯodos mostraram aumentos acentuados no
  tamanho e no nГәmero de interaГ§Гөes em comparaГ§ГЈo com redes de dois perГӯodos e de perГӯodo Гәnico, sugerindo uma partiГ§ГЈo tem-
  poral de nicho associada a nectГЎrios extraflorais. MudanГ§as composicionais tambГ©m foram detectadas por meio da diversidade
  beta de interaГ§Гөes. Uma maior substituiГ§ГЈo de espГ©cies e religaГ§ГЈo de interaГ§Гөes ocorreu entre o perГӯodo noturno e ambos os
  perГӯodos diurnos. Nossos resultados destacam o papel crucial da escala temporal diГЎria na estruturaГ§ГЈo dessas redes mutualГӯsti-
  cas e revelam como diferentes estratГ©gias de forrageamento das formigas dividem o recurso ao longo do tempo, alertando contra
  interpretaГ§Гөes ecolГіgicas derivadas exclusivamente de amostragem diurna.


1вҖҜвҖҜвҖҜ|вҖҜвҖҜвҖҜIntroduction                                                     In highly seasonal environments, daily fluctuations in the volume
                                                                         and concentration of extrafloral nectar have been documented for
Tropical forests are known for their high biodiversity, especially       several plant species (Anjos et al. 2016; Lange et al. 2017). Under
of arthropod species (Basset et al. 2015; De Aquino et al. 2022).        stronger daytime water stress, nectar tends to be more concen-

Silva et al. 2022), whose species interact with plants in different      resources to different consumers throughout the day (Chavarro-Вӯ
ways, playing important structuring roles (Del-ВӯClaro and Torezan-Вӯ      RodrГӯguez et al. 2013). Despite this evidence from seasonal sys-
Silingardi 2009; Del-ВӯClaro et al. 2018; Moura and Del-ВӯClaro 2023).     tems, no studies have yet investigated daily variation in antвҖ“plant
Ants, for example, play a role in seed dispersal (Fontenele and          interactions mediated by extrafloral nectaries in tropical forests.
Schmidt 2021), plant defence against herbivory (Pereira et al. 2020)     Nevertheless, a turnover of ant species exploiting these glands
and pollination (Delnevo et al. 2020). Among these, antвҖ“plant in-        over the daily cycle is likely to occur, as many tropical ant spe-

In tropical ecosystems, extrafloral nectaries constitute an abun-        adequately represented.
dant and widespread food resource, exploited by a large number
of ant species (DГЎttilo, Marquitti, et al. 2014; Nogueira et al. 2020;   To assess the daily dynamics of these antвҖ“plant interactions at

ciation is particularly prominent in Leguminosae, a plant family         Bascompte and Jordano 2007) and beta diversity of interactions
rich in species bearing extrafloral nectaries (Marazzi et al. 2019).     (Poisot et al. 2012; Ratoni et al. 2025). Complex networks have

2вҖҜвҖҜвҖҜ|вҖҜвҖҜвҖҜMethods                                                       Considering the phenological variation of EFN-Вӯbearing plants
                                                                      (FalcГЈo et al. 2016; Nogueira et al. 2020; Miranda et al. 2025),
2.1вҖҜвҖҜвҖҜ|вҖҜвҖҜвҖҜStudy Area                                                  we performed two samplings per plot, each including observa-
                                                                      tions in the morning, afternoon and night. The first sampling
Fieldwork was carried out in a forest fragment within the             occurred during the rainy season, from January to March 2024,
Catuaba Experimental Farm (10В°04вҖІ S, 67В°37вҖІ W), located in the        and the second during the dry season, from June to August 2024.
municipality of Senador Guiomard, state of Acre, in the south-        All observations were conducted by the same three trained ob-
western Brazilian Amazon. This forest fragment has an area            servers, experienced in detecting interactions between ants and
of 1166 ha and the vegetation type is open rainforest with palm       EFN-Вӯbearing plants in tropical forests, across all plots. During
trees and dense rainforest with bamboo (Silveira et al. 2020).        the dry season, samplings of each plot were carried out in the
The canopy height ranges from 20 to 40 m, with emergent trees         morning, afternoon and night on the same day (one plot per
reaching up to 45 m (Silveira et al. 2020). This region has a         day). During the rainy season, when it rained between sampling
monsoon climate (Am), with an average annual precipitation            periods, the observations were cancelled but resumed the fol-
of 1900вҖ“2200 mm, and two seasons (Alvares et al. 2014). The           lowing day immediately as an attempt to control for more pro-
dry season, with reduced average monthly precipitation, occurs        nounced climatic variations.
from June to August, and the rainy season from September to
May (Alvares et al. 2014). The average annual temperature is          We identified the ants to species or morphospecies using
24.5В°C, with a daily variation of Вұ7.5В°C (Silveira et al. 2020).      identification keys (Mackay and Mackay 2010; Ortiz and
                                                                      FernГЎndez 2011; Baccaro et al. 2015; Feitosa and Prada-Вӯ

and Processing                                                        et al. 2021; Feitosa and Dias 2024; FranГ§a et al. 2024) and by

We sampled antвҖ“plant interactions mediated by extrafloral             and specimens deposited at the Community Ecology Lab of the
nectaries in nine 150 Г— 10 m (1500 m2) plots. These plots were        Universidade Federal do Amazonas, where the voucher speci-
established every 500 m along a 5 km eastвҖ“west trail, following       mens were deposited.

a sampling plot, searching for EFN-Вӯbearing plants at heights         standard identification guides (Ribeiro et al. 1999; Pennington

Species delimitation was based on leaf and stem characteristics,      by chance given a partner's availability (BlГјthgen et al. 2006).

erence EFN-Вӯbearing plant specimens are provided in Table S2.         We used the NODF-Вӯmetric (Nestedness Based on Overlap and
The voucher specimens from our work are deposited in the              Decreasing Fill) (Almeida-ВӯNeto et al. 2008) to estimate the de-
LaboratГіrio de BotГўnica e Ecologia Vegetal of the Universidade        gree of nestedness in our networks with binary data, and the
Federal do Acre.                                                      WNODF-Вӯmetric (Weighted Nestedness Metric Based on Overlap
                                                                      and Decreasing Fill) (Almeida-ВӯNeto and Ulrich 2011) to estimate
                                                                      quantitative nestedness. In binary nestedness, all interactions
2.3вҖҜвҖҜвҖҜ|вҖҜвҖҜвҖҜData Analysis                                               are considered ecologically equivalent, and differences among
                                                                      them are ignored (Almeida-ВӯNeto and Ulrich 2011). Conversely,
We used an interaction network approach to investigate poten-         in quantitative nestedness, the weight of interactions is based
tial daily changes in the structural organisation of antвҖ“plant        on the abundances of the interacting partners (Almeida-ВӯNeto
interactions mediated by extrafloral nectaries. For this pur-         and Ulrich 2011). The values of both nestedness indices ranged
pose, we organised the sampled data into quantitative matri-          from 0 (non-Вӯnested) to 100 (perfectly nested). We calculated the
ces, in which the elements (aij) represented the frequency (i.e.,     NODF-Вӯmetric with the ANINHADO software (GuimarГЈes and
the number of times) with which plant species i interacted with       GuimarГЈes 2006), and we estimated the NODF significance
ant species j within the plot (Bascompte et al. 2003). For each       using p values based on Null Model II (1000 randomisations),
of the nine plots, we built a matrix of antвҖ“plant interaction         for which the probability of an interaction occurring is propor-
data sampled in the morning (M networks), another with data           tional to the observed number of interactions of both plant and
sampled in the afternoon (A networks), and a third with data          ant species (Bascompte et al. 2003). We estimated WNODF sig-
sampled at night (N networks), for a total of 27 matrices, each       nificance using p values based on a null model (1000 randomis-
representing a single-Вӯperiod network. To organise these matri-       ations), generated using the r2dtable algorithm (Patefield 1981),
ces, we combined data from samplings conducted during both            which holds the marginal totals constant (i.e., observed row
the rainy and dry seasons. We also built matrices for each plot,      and column totals) whilst allowing connectance to vary. We
containing accumulated data from two consecutive periods of           calculated diversity of interactions, network specialisation and
the day (two-Вӯperiod networks): morning and afternoon (M + A          WNODF using the вҖҳbipartiteвҖҷ package (Dormann et al. 2025) in
networks), afternoon and night (A + N networks), and night and        R software, version 4.4.1 (R Core Team 2024).
morning (N + M networks). In these matrices, we also combined
data collected during both the rainy and dry seasons, treating        We used generalised linear mixed models (GLMMs) (Bolker
the presence of the same ant species on a plant across two con-       et al. 2009) and linear mixed-Вӯ         effects models (LMMs)
secutive periods as a single interaction, yielding an additional 27   (Crawley 2012) to test whether the antвҖ“plant network descrip-
matrices. This combination of consecutive periods aimed to sim-       tors differed among the three periods of the day. We considered
ulate typical field sampling protocols that often focus on diurnal    each network descriptor (network size, number of interactions,
activity (morning + afternoon) or consecutive shifts, thereby         diversity of interactions, network specialisation and nestedness)
providing a more realistic basis for comparison. Finally, for each    calculated from the single-Вӯperiod networks (M, A and N net-
plot, we also constructed a matrix that accumulated data from         works) as a response variable, and the three periods of the day
all three periods of the day (M + A + N networks) and from both       (M, A and N) as an explanatory variable. We also included the
rainy and dry seasons, resulting in nine matrices with compre-        identity of each plot as random effects in the models to account
hensive data. In these matrices, we also considered the presence      for repeated measures within plots and to control for temporal
of the same ant species on a plant during three periods as a sin-     pseudoreplication. We tested the models' significance via analy-
gle interaction.                                                      sis of variance between the evaluated model and the null model
                                                                      (intercept and random effect only). Subsequently, we performed
In total, we built 63 quantitative matrices (i.e., 63 antвҖ“plant       contrast tests to verify pairwise differences among the three pe-
networks), and for each of them, we calculated five network de-       riods of the day. We also performed residual analysis and tests
scriptors to characterise antвҖ“plant interactions at different time    for overdispersion (Crawley 2012) to verify the adequacy of the
intervals: number of interactions, network size, diversity of in-     models' predictions. We examined the network size and num-
teractions, network specialisation and nestedness (using both         ber of interactions through a GLMM with Poisson error distri-
quantitative and binary data). The number of interactions rep-        bution, and the diversity of interactions, network specialisation
resents the total number of interactions observed in the network,     and nestedness (NODF and WNODF) through a LMM with
including all ant and plant species in a given matrix (DГЎttilo,       Gaussian error distribution.



4 of 15                                                                                                               Austral Ecology, 2026
                                                                                                                                                14429993, 2026, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/aec.70206 by William Magnusson - INPA - Instituto Nacional de Pesquisas da Amazonia , Wiley Online Library on [24/03/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
We employed a similar analytical scheme to investigate whether             interactions (ОІWN), species turnover (ОІST) and interaction rewir-
network descriptors varied across longer time intervals. For               ing (ОІOS) as response variables, and the different pairwise com-
these models, we considered each network descriptor calculated             binations of periods of the day (M and A, A and N, and N and
from the two-Вӯperiod networks as a response variable and the               M) as the explanatory variables. We also included the identity of
different time intervals (M + A, A + N and N + M) as explana-              each plot as random effects in the models to account for variance
tory variables. Finally, we calculated the increase or decrease            associated with random site effects. Subsequently, we performed
in network descriptor values from the three-Вӯperiod networks               contrast tests to verify pairwise differences between the ОІWN, ОІST
(M + A + N) relative to those calculated from the single-Вӯperiod           and ОІOS values calculated from different pairwise combinations
and two-Вӯperiod networks. We chose not to include the different            of periods of the day. We also performed residual analysis and
time intervals (M, A, N, M + A, A + N, N + M and M + A + N) in             tests for overdispersion (Crawley 2012) to verify the adequacy of
the same model, as their respective matrices were based on dif-            the models' predictions.
ferent sampling efforts.
                                                                           We created GLMMs and LMMs using the вҖҳlme4вҖҷ package (Bates
We also evaluated the constancy of the central core of highly              et al. 2025), conducted analysis of variance between each
interacting species among different periods of the day (M, A               model and the null model with the вҖҳveganвҖҷ package (Oksanen
and N). For this, we categorised ant and plant species accord-             et al. 2025), ran contrast tests using the вҖҳemmeansвҖҷ package
ing to DГЎttilo et al. (2013): G c = (k i вҲ’ k mean)/Пғk , where k i = mean   (Lenth et al. 2025) and assessed residuals of the GLMMs and
number of links for a given plant/ant species, k mean = mean               LMMs with the вҖҳDHARMaвҖҷ package (Florian et al. 2025) in R
number of links for all plant/ant species in the network, and              version 4.4.1 (R Core Team 2024).
Пғk = standard deviation of the number of links for plant/ant
species. Species with G c вүҘ 1 belong to the generalist core of
highly interacting species within the network, as they exhibit             3вҖҜвҖҜвҖҜ|вҖҜвҖҜвҖҜResults
a higher number of interactions compared to other species at
the same trophic level. In contrast, species with G c < 1 are lo-          In total, we recorded 86 ant species across 22 genera and 6 sub-
cated at the periphery of the network, as they exhibit a lower             families (Table S1). Of these, 30 morphospecies could not be
number of interactions compared to other species at the same               identified to the species level and were assigned to a unique mor-
trophic level.                                                             phospecies code. We recorded 87 EFN-Вӯbearing plant species,
                                                                           distributed in 25 genera and 18 families (Table S2). Of these, 76
To assess total beta diversity of interactions (ОІWN) across                morphospecies could not be identified to the species level (67 to
different periods of the day, we applied the framework pro-                the genus level, seven to the family level and two with their fam-
posed by Poisot et al. (2012). This index is derived from                  ilies undetermined). As with ant species, we assigned a unique
Whittaker's (1960) beta diversity index (ОІW ), which consid-               code to each plant morphospecies.
ers the number of shared species and the number of unique
species between two communities, and ranges from zero to                   We recorded 65 ant species and 80 EFN-Вӯbearing plant species in
one, where zero indicates that the two communities have                    the morning, 66 ant species and 81 EFN-Вӯbearing plant species
completely similar species and one indicates that they have                in the afternoon, and 55 ant species and 70 EFN-Вӯbearing plant
completely different species. The total beta diversity of inter-           species at night (Tables S1 and S2). Six ant species (Dolichoderus

tions and can be partitioned into two additive components:                 Pseudomyrmex gracilis Fabricius, 1804, Pseudomyrmex sp.3,

tions (ОІWN), through standardising the calculated values:                  S2). Finally, nine ant species (Dolichoderus imitator Emery,

tions are reassembled).                                                    Wasmannia sp.1) were observed interacting exclusively at night.

FIGURE 1вҖҜвҖҜвҖҜвҖҜ|вҖҜвҖҜвҖҜвҖҜBoxplot of antвҖ“plant network descriptors calculated using data from a single period of the day: Morning (M), Afternoon (A) and Night
(N), two periods of the day (M + A, A + N and N + M), and all three periods of the day (M + A + N), in nine plots of a tropical forest fragment located
in the state of Acre, Brazilian Amazon. The lines represent the minimum and maximum values, the box represents the first and third quartiles, and
the line within the box represents the median. The letters in the graphs correspond to the results of the pairwise comparisons. Identical letters within
the same analysis indicate no significant difference for the respective network descriptor among the periods or time intervals. In contrast, different
letters indicate statistically significant differences in pairwise comparisons. Lowercase and uppercase letters represent outcomes from different sta-
tistical tests.

A total of 12, 11 and 13 ant species constituted the central
core of the M, A and N networks, respectively. Among these,

We found that, for all pairwise combinations of periods of the
day, the interaction rewiring (ОІ OS) contributed more to the
additive partitioning of the total beta diversity of interactions
(ОІWN) than species turnover (ОІST) (Figure 3). We detected sig-
nificant differences in the total beta diversity of interactions
(ОІWN) among different pairwise combinations of periods of the
day (p < 0.001) (Figure 4). The total beta diversity of interac-
tions (ОІWN) was lower between M and A networks (0.32 Вұ 0.07)
than between A and N networks (0.46 Вұ 0.07) and N and M
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           Diversity of interaction
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      Network specialisation
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 Number of interaction




networks (0.46 Вұ 0.12) (Figure 4 and Table S4). However, we
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        Network descriptor




                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               Weighted nestedness




did not detect significant differences between the indices cal-
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     Binary nestedness




culated for the A and N networks and for the N and M net-
works (Figure 4 and Table S4). We also detected significant
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         Network size




differences in species turnover (ОІST) among different pair-
wise combinations of periods of the day (p < 0.001) (Figure 4).

(0.11 Вұ 0.03) than between A and N networks (0.19 Вұ 0.07) and

ble. The beta diversity of interactions also varied throughout the           by different plant species throughout the day (Anjos et al. 2016;
day, especially during the nocturnal period. These results sug-              Lange et al. 2017). In highly seasonal environments, some plant

the day. Thus, while global network properties such as special-              whereas others may secrete larger volumes at night (Lange

acting species and the configuration of their links are dynamic,             centration throughout the day may also result in greater ant at-

FIGURE 3вҖҜвҖҜвҖҜвҖҜ|вҖҜвҖҜвҖҜвҖҜProportion of additive partition of total beta diversity of interactions (ОІWN) recorded in three periods of the day (morning, afternoon
and night), in nine plots of a tropical forest fragment located in the state of Acre, Brazilian Amazon. Each bar represents the standardised value of
interaction turnover. In black, the species turnover generated by changes in species composition (ОІST); in grey, the interaction rewiring (ОІOS). The
values of the additive partition were determined by dividing each calculated value of ОІST and ОІOS by ОІWN. Graph вҖҳAвҖҷ is relative to the beta diversity of
interactions between morning and afternoon, вҖҳBвҖҷ between afternoon and night and вҖҳCвҖҷ between night and morning.

of the day (M and A, A and N, and N and M), in nine plots of a tropical forest fragment located in the state of Acre, Brazilian Amazon. The lines rep-
resent the minimum and maximum values, the box represents the first and third quartiles, and the line within the box represents the median. The
letters in the graphs correspond to the results of the pairwise comparisons. Identical letters in the same graph indicate no significant difference for
the respective index among the periods. In contrast, different letters indicate statistically significant differences in pairwise comparisons.

We detected substantial increases in network size and the num-                due to the greater daily temperature range of these ecosystems

revealing turnover of species throughout the day. Thus, ecolog-               to a single period. All networks showed one to two highly inter-

recorded, 24 (28%) were recorded exclusively in a single period               position involving at least one species. For EFN-Вӯbearing plants,

with low frequency in the networks. Changes between highly                    made cautiously, given that uncertainties in morphospecies de-

nectar (DГЎttilo, Fagundes, et al. 2014; Lange et al. 2017), in-
dicating strong interspecific competition over this resource.         We describe some aspects of the daily dynamics of antвҖ“plant in-
As discussed above, in our study, some numerically dominant           teractions mediated by extrafloral nectaries in a tropical forest of
ant species remained in the central cores of the networks             the Brazilian Amazon. The nested pattern of these interactions
throughout all three periods, exerting continuous competi-            remained stable throughout the day, possibly due to the per-
tive pressure on the other species. Among the peripheral ant          sistence of some dominant ant species on different EFN-Вӯbearing
species, more restricted to one or two periods, some genera           plants during all three periods. The network size and diversity
such as Camponotus, Pseudomyrmex and Paraponera have                  of interactions were greater in the morning and afternoon, indi-
aggressive species that tend to compete by the displacement           cating a higher number of species interacting during the diurnal
of competitors (HГ¶lldobler and Wilson 1990; Breed et al. 1991;        periods. Different factors may be triggering this pattern, such
EspГӯrito Santo et al. 2011; Barnett et al. 2014). Therefore, the      as the low daily temperature range caused by dense vegetation,
turnover of these ant species throughout the day, especially          temperature tolerance restrictions in some nocturnal ant spe-
at night, may reduce interspecific competition in plants not          cies, and variations in the volume and concentration of extra-
numerically dominated by the highly interactive ant species           floral nectar throughout the day among different plant species.
in network cores, favouring their coexistence. Together, these        We also detected an increase in network size and the number
patterns suggest that the nocturnal period acts as a stronger         of interactions when combining data across three periods, in-
ecological filter on the network, restricting participation to        dicating species turnover throughout the day, especially among
a smaller set of ant species while amplifying the relative in-        ants. Temporal niche partitioning at the daily scale may be a
fluence of competitively dominant and behaviourally special-          key mechanism by which ant communities reduce direct com-
ised taxa.                                                            petition while maintaining high levels of species coexistence in

VГЎzquez, D. P., N. BlГјuthgen, L. Cagnolo, and N. P. Chacoff. 2009.

1093/вҖӢaob/вҖӢmcp057.

Supporting Information
Additional supporting information can be found online in the Supporting
Information section. Table S1: List of ant species present in the antвҖ“
plant networks sampled in three different periods of the day in nine
plots of a tropical forest fragment located in the state of Acre, Brazilian
Amazon. The codes refer to the ant species that belong to the generalist
core of the networks. M = morning; A = afternoon; N = night. Table S2:
List of EFN-Вӯbearing plant species in the antвҖ“plant networks sampled in
three different periods of the day in nine plots of a tropical forest frag-
ment located in the state of Acre, Brazilian Amazon. The codes refer to
the EFN-Вӯbearing plant species that belong to the generalist core of the
networks. M = morning; A = afternoon; N = night. Table S3: Network
size, number of interactions, network specialisation, diversity of inter-
action and nestedness (WNODF and NODF) of the antвҖ“plant networks
sampled in different time intervals in nine plots of a tropical forest frag-
ment located in the state of Acre, Brazilian Amazon. M = morning net-
works, A = afternoon networks, N = night networks, M + A = networks
with accumulated morning and afternoon data, A + N = networks with
accumulated afternoon and night data, N + M = networks with accumu-
lated night and morning data, M + A + N = networks with accumulated
morning, afternoon and night data. Table S4: Network size, number of
interactions, network specialisation, diversity of interaction and nested-
ness (WNODF and NODF) of the antвҖ“plant networks sampled in differ-
ent time intervals in nine plots of a tropical forest fragment located in
the state of Acre, Brazilian Amazon. Table S5: Richness of ant species
